citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
Positive counterions, in order to avoid this central low-conductivity region, flow into the electric double layers at the microchannel walls where they counterbalance the wall charge. Such surface conduction is driven by the amplified axial electric field in the depleted region, which also pushes the negative co-ions, which remain in the central region of the channel, to the left. As a result, the concentration gradient becomes sharper with time, leading to a steady shock wave [76], which propagates from the membrane surface towards the left edge of the microchannel.
The physicochemistry of the phenomenon is quite interesting. Yaroshchuk [175] carried out an analysis of pattern of propagating deionization shock in terms of local thermodynamic equilibrium in the approximation of local electric neutrality in virtual solution. In particular, he has shown that the deionization-shock pattern is really pronounced only at quite high steady-state voltages.
Propagation of deionization shock in a microstructure of channels with charged walls, such as a porous frit, may be used for water deionization [76,77].

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
In Fig. 13b, the water flows from top to bottom through a porous frit. The current flows in the same direction towards a CEM, which is at the bottom. The "deionization shock" propagates through the microstructure (from the membrane at the bottom to the top), leaving behind an ultrapure solution, nearly devoid of co-ions and colloidal impurities.

7. Overlimiting current ED
In conventional ED, the limiting current density is usually attained at about 1 V per cell pair [159]. In most cases of practical use of ED, this or a slightly lower voltage is steadily applied. The increase in voltage leads to some undesirable effects: growing electric consumption, lowering current efficiency, increasing scaling and fouling. However, the use of overlimiting current modes attracts a number of researchers and engineers [23,28,125,176,177]. The interest is in the fact that the overlimiting mass transfer rate at reasonable voltages close to 2 V may be up to 3 times higher than i_lim [159]. This means that even if the costs related to electric consumption increases, the total costs per 1 m^3 of treated water may be lower owing to decreasing capital costs since lesser amount of membranes and membrane devices needed.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
Besides, smaller sizes of the membrane installation may give other advantages, especially in production facilities of limited dimensions.
To reduce the risk of scaling and fouling, several methods may be used. The first one is the pre-treatment of water prior to ED. The elimination of salts providing hardness of the water excludes the cause of salt deposition on the membrane surface. Another way is the application of membrane pairs where the water splitting rate is higher at the CEM surface than at the AEM one. In this case, the desalinating stream becomes alkalized while the concentrating stream becomes acid [177]. The latter prevents the deposition of carbonates and oxides on the concentrating membrane surface. The alkalinization of desalinating stream increases the degree of weak-acid anion ionization that improves the removal of weak acids (such as silicic, boric and other) from feed water. The third way is the use of pulsed current mode. This mode not only allows mitigation of scaling and fouling, but also results in an increasing rate of ED desalination.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
Mishchuk [26] argues that the gain in applying pulsed currents may be due to electroconvection mixing, which continues during the pause owing to inertial properties of liquid movement. This residual convection could partially restore the electrolyte concentration near the membrane.
The variation in pH may be a desirable effect in the case where ampholyte (such as organic acids) containing solutions are treated and a shift in pH results in obtaining the needed product [178–180]. In this case often bipolar membranes and overlimiting current densities are used in order to obtain controlled generation of H+ and OH- ions.
The application of overlimiting currents in ED is in accordance with rather general trend of process intensification in chemical engineering. However, as it was rightly noted by Strathmann [1], this emerging mode of ED process is under development and needs further study.

8. Continuous electrodeionization (CEDI)
To obtain high purity water, electrodialyzers with a special stack design are applied, the process is called continuous electrodeionization (CEDI) [2,181].

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
These apparatuses are able to produce deionized water, the resistivity of which is close to that of pure water (18 Mohm cm at 25 °C). The process design and the different hardware components needed in CEDI are very similar to those used in conventional ED. The main difference is that in a CEDI stack the diluate cells and sometimes also the concentrate cells are filled with an ion exchange filler: a bed of ion-exchange resin granules [152,159,182,183], ion-exchange textile, [184,185], conducting spacer [182,186–189] or organic porous ion exchange material [190]. The stacks with profiled (relief/undulated) ion-exchange membranes [28,66,152,165] occupy an intermediate position between CEDI with ion-exchange bed and conventional ED with smooth membranes and non-conductive spacer. The range of feed solutions with TDS from 5 to 500 mg L^{-1} (10^{-4} to 10^{-2} M NaCl) is the most effective field of profiled membranes application.
The ion-exchange resin increases the conductivity in the diluate cells; as a result, the resistance of CEDI stack is significantly lowered.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content: However, the main difference from conventional ED lies in the mechanism of deionization: the electro-migration transfer of salt ions in CEDI is combined with ion exchange: the salt ions are exchanged with the H+ and OH- ions present in the ion-exchange material [66,176,182]. The latter enter ion-exchange resins or membranes due to water splitting reaction [17,191] occurring at A=C contacts where A is an anion- and C is a cation-exchange material, the arrow shows the direction of the current flowing (Fig. 14). Thus, continuous regeneration of ion-exchanger by the products of the water dissociation occurs allowing deep demineralization of the feed water. The active mass exchange surface in a CEDI stack is much higher than that in an ED one due to the presence of ion-exchange bed. The ion-exchange can occur on the all ion-exchange particle surface including the part normal to the current flow direction thus enhancing the rate and the depth of deionization.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
Besides, ion-exchange particles or profile on the membrane surface contribute to improving hydrodynamics and reducing the diffusion layer thickness.
The source of H+ and OH- ions in CEDI can not only be the A=C contacts between ion-exchange fillers, but also bipolar membranes [1,152,181] or electrochemical reactions occurring at the electrodes [192].
It can be expected that the geometry of CEDI stacks promotes electroconvection. Really, ion-exchange beads, projections and cavities provide curved surface where conditions favorable for Dukhin's electroconvection (Figs. 3 and 11) are realized. Apparently, electroconvection, water splitting and ion exchange (regeneration of ion-exchanger) should interfere between them. However, as far as we know there are no studies where electroconvection would be taken into account in CEDI process.
In ED of dilute solutions under intensive currents, at least, when using profiled membranes, the electrical conductivity and diffusion permeability of ion exchange membranes no longer play decisive role. More important are surface properties such as ability of water splitting, hydrophobicity, electric and geometric surface heterogeneity (see Section 5). Hence, optimization of membrane surface is the main issue in this field.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
9. Capacitive deionization with ion exchange membranes
Membrane capacitive deionization (MCDI) is a newly emerging water desalination technique [1–4,172,193–200] based on applying a cell voltage between two oppositely placed porous electrodes sandwiching a spacer channel that transports the water to be desalinated. In MCDI, ion-exchange membranes are positioned in front of each porous electrode to prevent co-ions from leaving the electrode region during ion adsorption, thereby enhancing the salt adsorption capacity.
The principles of the process involve phenomena occurring in the electrical double layer at electrodes and membranes, hence, charging and discharging of these layers and the ion transport inevitably coupled with water transport. From this point of view the phenomena of concentration polarization and electroconvection are also involved, which provides similarity with overlimiting current transfer in ED. The details and issues of this promising technique may be found in reviews [193,194,199] and original publications [172,195–198].

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
10. Performances of electrodialysis in intensive current regimes
As it was shown above, generally, with increasing current density in an ED process the mass transfer rate increases. This results in a lower membrane area required for desalination, and, consequently, in lower investment costs. However, higher current density involves higher energy costs. The latter is mainly due to increasing voltage, as the total current roughly does not change if the feed and product concentrations are fixed, because it is determined by the number of electrolyte moles, which are removed [1]. Thus, the total costs of ED desalination are a function of the applied voltage. As well, the current efficiency and permeselectivity depend also on the current density/voltage. Generally, the choice of current density/voltage is a question of process optimization.

10.1. Cost estimation of ED desalination as a function of current density
Usually, the optimum current density in ED desalination is considered to be about 80–90% of the limiting current density value; the voltage drop per cell pair being in the range of 0.5 to 1.0 V, increasing with increasing the distance between the membranes [2,201].

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
In the cost estimation, the following costs are taken into account: the operating costs, which include energy and maintenance costs as well as the costs of the membrane replacement; and the capital investment costs, which are necessary to run the ED plant in operation [2].
The costs are calculated for an ED unit with 40 cm width of working membrane area (a). The length of the working membrane area (L) is determined by the required degree of desalination (γ), which is chosen in our calculations to be 80%. With increasing current density, the value of L decreases resulting in lower investment costs, however, the energy costs grows [2].
The electrical power required for the ion transfer in a desalination process, P_des, is given by the total current, I, passing through the stack multiplied by the applied voltage [2]: P_des = I N_cell U_cell = (F (c_in - c_out) W_cell / ξ) N_cell U_cell, where W_cell is the flow rate of the solution in the diluate cell, c_in and c_out are the equivalent concentrations of the diluate at the cell inlet and outlet, respectively, ξ is the current efficiency, U_cell is the voltage per cell pair, and N_cell is the number of cell pairs in the stack.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
The values of the current, the current efficiency and the length of the cell are found by using experimental data presented in Ref. [126]. The data [126] were obtained for a cell with 3 cm width and 10 cm length of working membrane area. Electrodialysis was realized in batch quasi-steady-state mode: diluate recirculated through an intermediate tank where the pH value was maintained constant (equal to 7) during the experiment run.
The mass transfer coefficient k was found by the rate of NaCl concentration decrease in the tank. The value of ξ is taken equal to the effective transport number of Cl ions in the AEM, which controls the rate of desalination since the water splitting at this membrane was higher than that at the cation-exchange one. The pH of diluate increased when passing through the cell, and NaOH was added in the tank to keep its pH equal to 7. k and ξ as functions of the NaCl concentration at the cell inlet (equal to the concentration in the tank) are shown in Fig. 15. The data were obtained for two stacks. The first one was composed of Russian commercial heterogeneous membranes MK-40 and MA-40 separated by a 1 mm thickness net spacer (S). The flow velocity in the diluate compartment was 2.5 cm s^{-1}.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
The second stack contained a MK-40/Nf membrane obtained from the MK-40 by coating its surface with a thin film of Nafion, and a MA-40M membrane obtained from the MA-40 by its chemical treatment in order to graft on the surface quaternary anion-exchange groups, which have low catalytic activity towards water splitting reaction. The same spacer and the same flow velocity were used.
The value of k is linked with the partial current density of Cl- ions, i_Cl-, across the anion-exchange membrane according to the following equation: k = i_Cl- / (F c_in). Eq. (20) can be considered as the definition of k [126]. Combining Eq. (20) with the right-hand side of Eq. (19) and taken into account that i_Cl- = i ξ, we find c_out = c_in (1 - kS/W_cell). The total current flowing through the cell is I = k F c_in S / ξ.
To calculate the length of the path needed to obtain the required desalination degree (80% in the considered case), the compartmentation method was used. The idea of this method consists of the presentation of a long channel as a sequence of short ones, the outlet concentration for an element number j is considered as the inlet one for j+1 element [156,202].

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content: In the case of electrodialysis, the potential drop per cell pair does not vary with the distance from the entrance. This circumstance facilitates the application of the method: instead of studying behavior of long ED cells, it is sufficient to study a "short" cell with a fixed length under conditions where the inlet concentration changes with time. It is necessary, however, that the peculiarities of the inlet region (such as non-uniform distribution of velocity, development of the diffusion layer thickness) can be neglected. It was found experimentally [156] that a cell with 10 cm of the membrane working length satisfies the condition above. Thus, Eq. (21) can be employed for the first cell segment of 10 cm length to calculate the outlet concentration when knowing the inlet concentration and the related value of k (Fig. 15); Eq. (22) can be used to find the total current through the first segment. Then considering the found concentration as the inlet one for the next 10 cm segment, Eqs. (21) and (22) can be used again together with the data of Fig. 15 and so on.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
The length of the last segment should be fitted (via changing S in Eq. (21)) in order to arrive at the final concentration corresponding to the required degree of desalination. The values of the total length, which is needed to have 80% degree of desalination of a 0.012 M NaCl solution, as a function of the voltage per cell pair, are given in Table 1. The outlet concentration is 0.0024 M.
To calculate the costs, a hypothetic stack containing 28 cell pairs needed to produce 1 m^3/h of diluate is considered. Its width a is taken 40 cm, and the length L is found as described above. The following additional characteristics are taken from the paper by Turek [201]: the working membrane area is 70% of the total area; the price of commercial membranes is $40/m^2, the total investment costs are $240/m^2 membrane and the maintenance costs (without membrane replacement) are 25% of the investment costs; the energy cost is $0.1/kWh. We assume that the stack's life is 12 years, and during this period, the membranes are replaced every 4 years. We did not take into account the energy costs needed for pumping.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
Fig. 16 shows the ED desalination costs as functions of the applied voltage per cell pair. The investment and maintenance costs including the membrane replacement cost as well as the costs of energy consumption for current passage are presented. As expected, in the case of commercial heterogeneous membranes, the minimum costs (about $0.17 /m^3, which is quite close to the values found by Turek [201]) relate to the current density slightly lower than the limiting current. At lower currents, the investments costs strongly increase while at higher currents, the energy costs are high. However, in the case of modified membranes, the price of which is assumed $60 /m^2, the minimum costs (equal to $0.15 /m^3) are shifted to a higher voltage close to 2.5 V/cell pair. It is due to lower investment costs in the case of modified membranes in despite of their higher price. The difference between the costs in both cases should be even more, if we took into account the pumping energy costs, which are roughly proportional to the membrane area.
Note that the electric energy costs are proportional to the power consumption. Thus, the electric energy costs equal to $0.1 /m^3 relate to the power consumption 1 kWh/m^3.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
Higher effects are expected in the case of profiled membranes, which remain effective even at quite low feed concentrations (<5·10^{-3} M) where the flat membranes with spacer show decreased mass transfer rate due to high imbalance in water splitting at AEM and CEM. According to [54], in a 20 cm in length stack with profiled membranes, at a flow velocity 2.9 cm s^{-1} and 2.5 V per cell pair, the Sherwood (Sh) number is equal to 48, when the inlet NaCl concentration is 1.1·10^{-5} M. The mass transfer coefficient, which is related to Sh as k = Sh D1/h [54], is then 2.8 dm h^{-1}. Here D1 is the counterion diffusion coefficient controlling the mass transfer rate in the cell, it was taken [54] equal to 1.3·10^{-5} cm^2 s^{-1} (for Na+).
The data presented in Fig. 16 show that the gain in mass transfer rate increases with diluting feed solution, which is apparently due to increasing role of electroconvection. Hydrophobization of cation-exchange membrane surface and lowering water splitting at the anion-exchange membrane result in essential enhancement of mass transfer. However, in the case of flat membranes in the concentration range >0.02 M NaCl, the mass transfer rate does not depend on whether the membrane was modified or not.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
The main factor controlling the mass transfer in this case is the electrolyte diffusion in the diffusion layer, whose the thickness, δ, is determined by the properties of the spacer and the flow velocity (the Reynolds number). In the case of the spacer used in experiment [126] and the velocity 2.5 cm s^{-1}, the value of δ related to k = 1.1 dm h^{-1} at c_in > 0.02 M, is about 0.1 mm. This value may be found from the relations k = Sh D1/h and Sh = 2h/δ.
The analysis made above shows that modification of membrane surface allows increasing mass transfer rate in ED stack. That shifts the optimum voltage of ED process in a higher range in comparison with conventional stacks. The considered examples prove that the ED operation in intensive current regimes can be economically advantageous. However, with increasing voltage, water splitting increases and after a certain voltage this process noticeably deteriorates the performance of electrodialysis, especially if the rate of water dissociation is different at the cation-exchange and the anion-exchange membranes.

10.2. Specific permselectivity
Not only the performance of ED desalination, but the characteristics of some other ED processes are functions of voltage/current density.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
Ion fractionation by electrodialysis is used to separate singly charged and multicharged ions. It is applied in electrodialytic concentration of seawater to produce sodium chloride [203,204], in denitrification of potable water [205,206], in separation of nutrient ions and organic compounds from salts [207,208] and in other separation processes such as separation of specific cations from the mixtures in hydrometallurgy [203].
Generally, the permselectivity among ions of the same sign of charge through an IEM is governed by the difference in affinity of the ions with the membrane (ion-exchange equilibrium constant) and the difference in the ion migration velocity in the membrane (mobility ratio among the ions) [203]. There are several approaches to membrane preparation resulting in selective permeation of singly charged ions through the membrane from a mixture with multicharged ions. This specific selectivity may be due to sieving of monovalent ions by a tight surface (e.g., polypyrrole [203]) layer, or due to stronger electrostatic repulsion force of the fixed cationic charge in a polyelectrolyte layer on the surface of a cation-exchange membrane [203], or due to the effect of hydrophilicity of anion-exchange membranes on permselectivity of anions [209].

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content: It is well known that membrane systems exhibit a high specific permselectivity only at low current densities: this property deteriorates with increasing current density[203,207–212]. The reason is in peculiar concentration polarization occurring in this case. Indeed, the concentration of the selectively permeated ion in the diffusion layers adjacent to the membrane changes more than that of the ion, which permeates with difficulty. It is lower at the depleted membrane surface and higher at the enriched surface, in comparison with the concentration of the poorly permeating ion. In these conditions, the concentration gradients of two competitive ions in the membrane are directed in opposite sides: the diffusion in the membrane is opposite to the migration flux and reduces the total flux of the selectively permeated ion, while it enhances the migration flux of the poorly permeating ion. With increasing current density, this effect increases. When the current density approaches its limiting values, the fluxes of competing counterions are no longer dependent on the membrane properties: they are determined by the transport in the depleted diffusion layer, the partial current density of competing counterion k, i_k_lim, can be calculated as follows: i_k_lim = (F/δ) D_k z_k c_k^I (1 + z_k/z_3), where c_k^I is the ion concentration in the depleting solution bulk, z_3 is the charge number of the co-ion. The role of the membrane is reduced in this case to the retention of co-ions.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
11. Conclusion
Due to Frilette, since 1957 it is known that the salt counterion transfer through an IEM is not saturated when the current density reaches its limiting value; the useful mass transfer continues to grow at i > i_lim. However, the mechanism of this growth was not sufficiently clear during decades. Even about 10 years ago, there were discussions as to what type of the current induced convection is more probable in the overlimiting current range: gravitational convection or electroconvection. Now it is admitted that in dilute solutions, the main mechanism of electrodialysis enhancement is electroconvection; the issues under consideration are the details of occurring this phenomenon. In addition to the modes of electroconvection suggested by Dukhin and Mishchuk and by Rubinstein and Zaltzman, some other kinds of coupling between ion and water transport are studying. Essential progress is obtained in mathematical description of overlimiting electrodialysis, which gives important information about the vortex structure and its dependence on the solution forced flow between the membranes. Promising desalination devices, which use the effects of ion and water transport coupling are proposed.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
It is found that the overlimiting transfer is strongly dependent on the membrane surface properties. By modifying IEM properties, it is possible to enhance the overlimiting electrodialysis. It is shown that ED operation in intensive current regimes with modified membranes can be economically advantageous. In this case, the optimum voltage of ED desalination corresponding to minimum ED costs is shifted from 0.8-1 V range justified for conventional membranes into a higher voltage range. However, with increasing voltage, water splitting increases leading to decreasing current efficiency and suppressing electroconvection. Thus after a certain voltage, the performances of ED desalination process are noticeably deteriorated. On the other hand, controlled water splitting can be a desirable process, especially when treating organic acids. Together with monopolar membranes, in these cases conventional and asymmetric bipolar membranes are used.
The here considered examples of overlimiting ED desalination realized in conventional and novel devices are far from optimum. Their optimization should go through enhancement of membrane surface properties (surface hydrophobization and relief geometry optimization) as well as through the choice of electric current regime.

citation.title: Desalination at overlimiting currents: State-of-the-art and perspectives

content:
We hope that this review allows better understanding of electromembrane desalination processes, which can be considered as derivatives of conventional electrodialysis: continuous electro-deionization, capacitive deionization, reverse electrodialysis and some others.
This brief review shows that there are many exciting possibilities to enhance water desalination process, which is constantly developing from Biblical times [213] to our days. Even now there are a lot of methods to make fresh water less costly and more available for people. We believe that in future scientific progress will bring even more interesting opportunities.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2014.01.008.
